index.guidance: For suspected TIA within 7 days: completion of the BPAC TIA decision support tool is required to confirm CT Head appropriate. For first seizure, MRI is the preferred first-line investigation where available.
index.guidanceNarrative: For clinically suspected anterior circulation TIA events, if the patient is a potential candidate for carotid endarterectomy, carotid angiography (CTA) or if unavailable carotid ultrasound should be added to the CT head exam.
index.alternativeManagement: Seizures with risk factors for intracranial pathology — seek Neurology phone advice. Poorly controlled or recurrent seizures — follow the appropriate pathway (Epilepsy in Adults, Epilepsy in Women, First Seizure in Adults, Seizure in Palliative Care HealthPathways).
index.notFundedDetail: Established advanced dementia of more than 2 years duration, no unexpected change in disease progression and establishing the subtype will not alter management.
index.footnotes:
*1 High risk features for TIA: less than 59 years old; ABCD2 greater than 4; crescendo TIAs; atrial fibrillation; on anticoagulants; severe or prolonged deficit (hemiparesis longer than 60 minutes); known carotid artery stenosis.
*2 Features suggestive of underlying SOL, bleed, or hydrocephalus: rapid cognitive decline over weeks; presence of focal neurological signs; history of falls; anticoagulant therapy; gait ataxia or incontinence; history of cancers with propensity to brain metastases (lung, melanoma, breast).
*3 Criteria for low-risk first seizure — all of the following must apply: single seizure which is unprovoked or provoked by a known substance; patient has since returned to normal health; none of the following risk factors for intracranial pathology apply: head injury, aged older than 40, fever/systemic symptoms, history of anticoagulation or malignancy, new focal neurologic deficit, focal seizure, persistent altered mental state, persistent headache.
*4 Risk factors for intracranial pathology: head injury; aged older than 40; fever/systemic symptoms; history of anticoagulation or malignancy; new focal neurologic deficit; focal seizure; persistent altered mental state; persistent headache.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Established advanced dementia >2 years, no unexpected change, subtype will not alter management — not routinely funded. Seizures with risk factors for intracranial pathology — seek Neurology phone advice rather than CT.

index.guidance: Reserve for patients where after initial investigations the GP remains strongly suspicious of malignancy but no focal pathology or biopsy site is identified. Two or more persistently abnormal bloods (Hb, CRP, Ca, ALP) significantly increases probability of cancer.
index.guidanceNarrative: This pathway covers patients with nonspecific symptoms where the GP suspects underlying primary or secondary malignancy in the abdomen or pelvis, with no localising features or mass for tissue sampling evident on examination or initial investigations. The presence of two or more persistently abnormal blood test results (Hb, CRP, Ca, ALP) significantly increases the probability of cancer being diagnosed. CT CAP should be reserved for patients where after initial investigations and review the GP remains strongly suspicious of underlying malignancy. For presentations with nonspecific symptoms and abnormal lab results not meeting criteria, discussion with PCRL or secondary care specialist or radiologist for consideration of CT imaging is recommended.
index.alternativeManagement: Not indicated where: patient has a current cancer diagnosis and is under continued follow-up; patient has been investigated by secondary care within the last 12 months for the same symptoms/signs; presentation requires urgent admission or urgent secondary care assessment; localising clinical features or results from preliminary investigations suggest cancer in a specific system; recent ultrasound of abdomen and pelvis within the last three months (seek advice from radiologist or secondary care specialist); CT Chest, Abdomen and Pelvis within the last 12 months (seek advice from radiologist or secondary care specialist).
index.notFundedDetail: Patient is unfit for treatment or unwilling to have further investigations and/or treatment.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not indicated where: current cancer under follow-up; investigated by secondary care within 12 months for same symptoms; localising features on initial investigations; recent USS abdomen/pelvis within 3 months; CT CAP within 12 months.

items.label: Male >50 or female >60 with strong suspicion of malignancy, no focal pathology, AND unintentional unexplained weight loss >5% over 3–6 months (+/- abdominal pain, fatigue, nausea)
items.short: Male >50 / female >60, unexplained weight loss >5%, strong malignancy suspicion

items.label: Male >40 or female >50 with strong suspicion of malignancy, no focal pathology, AND weight loss >5% over 3–6 months AND 2+ of: raised CRP, low Hb, raised Ca, high platelets, high ALP, low albumin — persistent on repeat after 3 weeks
items.short: Male >40 / female >50, weight loss >5%, 2+ abnormal bloods persisting

index.guidance: Urgent CXR (same or next day) remains the initial investigation where lung cancer is suspected. CT Chest is a subsequent step. A normal CXR does not exclude lung cancer (sensitivity ~75–80%).
index.guidanceNarrative: A diagnosis of lung cancer and initiating imaging investigations should be considered for all patients with concerning symptoms or signs lasting more than 3 weeks, or sooner if the patient has risk factors for lung cancer. Patients who identify as Māori have higher rates of lung cancer than non-Māori and a lower threshold for investigating concerning symptoms or signs should be considered especially if the patient has risk factors. Urgent CXR (same or next day) remains the initial investigation of choice for symptomatic patients where lung cancer is suspected. Studies suggest the sensitivity of a CXR is around 75-80%, highlighting the importance of safety netting for symptomatic patients with a normal CXR. The presence of multiple signs and symptoms, particularly in combination with haemoptysis, weight loss or persistent thrombocytosis, has a significantly stronger positive predictive value for lung cancer. Regarding CT after normal CXR: it is not yet clear how or when to select patients for CT in the presence of a normal CXR due to the low predictive value of symptoms. The overall incidence of lung cancer amongst CXR referrals is approximately 1.4%. Studies show the risk can be stratified using patient age and smoking status — current-smokers aged over 55 years are proposed as the threshold for CT after normal CXR.
index.alternativeManagement: Patient re-presents with concerning symptoms and CXR is normal but doesn't meet CT criteria — seek advice from secondary care respiratory team regarding appropriate management and whether CT chest is warranted. Persistent non-specific non-localising symptoms concerning for underlying cancer (e.g. weight loss, fatigue) and initial investigations including CXR and bloods fail to identify a cause — see CT Chest/Abdomen/Pelvis HealthPathway. Initial presentation with respiratory symptoms or signs concerning for lung cancer — refer for urgent CXR as the initial investigation. Follow-up of nodule discovered incidentally on CT chest scan — refer to secondary care for management advice unless radiology report confirms benign.
index.footnotes:
*1 Concerning symptoms and signs for lung cancer: unexplained new or worsening dyspnoea; unexplained cough persisting and worsening for more than 3 weeks; unexplained chest and/or shoulder pain persisting for more than 3 weeks; unexplained changes in existing symptoms in patients with underlying chronic respiratory problems persisting for more than 3 weeks; unexplained hoarseness persisting for more than 3 weeks; recurrent or unresolved chest infections; persistent unexplained haemoptysis in the absence of red flags; unexplained weight loss and loss of appetite persisting for more than 3 weeks; unexplained abnormal chest signs persisting more than 3 weeks; new supraclavicular lymphadenopathy; new cervical lymphadenopathy persisting more than 3 weeks where nodes are greater than 1cm in a patient with other lung cancer symptoms/signs/risk factors; features suggestive of paraneoplastic syndromes.
*2 Risk factors for lung cancer: current or past smoker (increased risk with duration and quantity; reduced risk with duration since quitting especially more than 15 years); family history of lung cancer in sibling; personal history of cancer especially head and neck cancer; history of lung disease including COPD, cystic fibrosis; exposure to asbestos, radon, air pollutants.
*3 Safety netting after normal CXR: with sensitivity of CXR for lung cancer reported as 75-80%, a normal CXR may provide false reassurance. Safety netting is a diagnostic management strategy to ensure patients are monitored until symptoms are explained or resolved. Persistent or worsening unexplained respiratory symptoms, especially clusters of symptoms and especially in patients at increased risk of lung cancer, warrant further investigation despite a normal CXR by way of referral for CT or discussion with a secondary care clinician or PCRL.
*4 Local pathways — direct to PET-CT: where CXR findings are suspicious for lung cancer but no features suggest it is not curable, the preferred next step in some districts is to proceed directly with whole body PET-CT rather than CT chest.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Initial presentation with respiratory symptoms: refer for urgent CXR first. Re-presentation with normal CXR but not meeting CT criteria: seek advice from secondary care respiratory team.

index.guidance: CTC may be appropriate where the patient is over 80, has co-morbidities making colonoscopy higher risk, has an abdominal mass, or following failed/incomplete colonoscopy. All referrals should go via secondary care single point of entry for triage to colonoscopy or CTC.
index.guidanceNarrative: The Ministry of Health and Bowel Cancer Working Group recommendation is for all referrals for investigation of bowel symptoms meeting the direct access referral criteria to be referred to secondary care via a single point of entry and triaged to either colonoscopy or CT colonography (CTC). CTC may be appropriate where the patient is over 80 years, has significant co-morbidities where colonoscopy presents higher risk (e.g. respiratory risk from sedation), has an abdominal mass on examination, or following failed or incomplete colonoscopy. CTC is of comparable sensitivity to colonoscopy for the detection of clinically significant polyps and tumours. All referrals require: ability to tolerate bowel prep and the procedure and possible further treatment, and no previous colonoscopy or CTC within the last 5 years unless there is a clear indication to repeat.
index.alternativeManagement: Rectal bleeding aged less than 50 years (normal haemoglobin) — consider secondary care referral for assessment and possible flexible sigmoidoscopy.
index.notFundedDetail: Acute diarrhoea for less than six weeks duration where it most likely has an infectious aetiology. Irritable bowel syndrome. Constipation as a single symptom. Uncomplicated CT-proven diverticulitis without suspicious radiological features. Abdominal pain alone without P3 six-week category features. Low ferritin with normal haemoglobin in a patient aged younger than 50 years.
index.footnotes:
*1 NZGG family history categories:
Category 2 (moderately increased familial risk): one first-degree relative diagnosed with CRC under age 55, OR two first-degree relatives on the same side of the family with CRC at any age.
Category 3 (potentially high risk): family history of FAP, Lynch syndrome or other familial CRC syndromes; one first-degree relative plus two or more first- or second-degree relatives all on the same side with CRC at any age; two first-degree relatives or one first-degree relative plus one or more second-degree relatives all on the same side with CRC, and one such relative was diagnosed under age 54, or developed two or more bowel cancers, or developed an extracolonic tumour suggestive of Lynch syndrome (endometrial, ovarian, stomach, small bowel, renal pelvis, pancreas or brain); a first-degree relative with CRC diagnosed under age 50 where tumour immunohistochemistry has revealed loss of protein expression for one of the mismatch repair genes.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not indicated for: acute diarrhoea <6 weeks (likely infectious); IBS; constipation as single symptom; uncomplicated CT-proven diverticulitis; abdominal pain alone; low ferritin with normal Hb aged <50. All criteria require ability to tolerate bowel prep AND no colonoscopy/CTC within last 5 years (unless clear indication).

index.guidance: CT IVU is preferred for renal masses, painless haematuria, and obstruction. Contraindicated if eGFR <30 or iodinated contrast allergy — use renal ultrasound instead. CT KUB is a separate non-contrast scan for stone detection.
index.guidanceNarrative: CT IVU (also known as CT Urogram) is the best imaging investigation for renal masses, investigating painless haematuria, and causes of obstruction, but this is at the expense of increased radiation dose and the need for intravenous contrast. Images are obtained in 2 or 3 phases to image the kidneys in the nephrogenic phase and ureters and bladder in excretory phases. CT IVU together with cystoscopy should be used for older patients (40 years or older) with painless haematuria and in younger patients (less than 40 years) when initial investigations including ultrasound and cystoscopy are abnormal or if haematuria persists. CT IVU uses a high dose of iodine contrast media which can be nephrotoxic. CT IVU is not recommended for patients with reduced renal function (eGFR lower than 30) or with known allergy to iodinated contrast — renal ultrasound is the recommended investigation in these patient groups. CT KUB is a different non-contrast scan, primarily for detection and monitoring of stones.

index.guidance: For pregnant patients or females <35 without haematuria, fever, history of stones, or solitary kidney: request renal tract ultrasound instead. Consider plain X-ray KUB at same time. Avoid repeated CT KUB in otherwise healthy patients aged <50 with known stones and typical presentation without red flags.
index.guidanceNarrative: For pregnant patients or females younger than 35 without haematuria, fever, history of stones, or solitary kidney, request renal tract ultrasound instead of CT KUB. Consider arranging additional plain X-ray KUB at the same time — confirming the stone is visible on x-ray at presentation enables 4-week follow-up with plain x-ray if required. Avoid requesting repeated CT KUB imaging in otherwise healthy patients aged younger than 50 years with a known history of kidney stones; if typical presentation without red flags, repeat imaging is not usually required.
index.alternativeManagement: Females aged younger than 35 years with renal colic — arrange renal ultrasound rather than CT KUB, unless the patient has: haematuria x10, fever, history of stones, or solitary kidney. Pregnant women with renal colic — arrange renal ultrasound.
index.notFundedDetail: Recurrent renal colic — imaging is not required if pain is well controlled and the patient is aged younger than 50 years, presenting with typical renal colic, has no red flags, and has had a confirmed stone on CT KUB during the last 5 years. Surveillance of asymptomatic patients with previous nephrolithiasis. Children younger than 16 years — consider requesting paediatric assessment.
index.footnotes: *1 Renal colic red flags: fever/rigors; single kidney; known significant renal impairment; suspected bilateral obstructing stones; pregnancy.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: recurrent renal colic in patients aged <50 with typical presentation, no red flags, and confirmed stone on CT KUB in last 5 years; surveillance of asymptomatic patients with previous nephrolithiasis; children younger than 16 years.

index.guidance: CRS symptoms are poor predictors of sinus disease on imaging (specificity ~30%). Early CT sinus is more cost-efficient than presuming CRS on symptoms alone. All prerequisites below must be met.
index.guidanceNarrative: Chronic Rhinosinusitis (CRS) symptoms and its clinical presentation to primary care have been shown to be poor predictors of sinus disease on imaging with a low specificity of approximately 30%. Early CT sinus to confirm the diagnosis has been shown to be both beneficial to the patient and more cost-efficient than presuming a diagnosis of CRS based on symptoms alone. Rhinosinusitis is one of the most common reasons for adult outpatient antibiotic prescriptions, though there is little clinical evidence to support this practice especially for chronic rhinosinusitis. These national CT sinus referral pre-requisites are necessarily based on a compromise between specialist advice and Health New Zealand funded resource availability. Refer for acute assessment without imaging if rhinosinusitis symptoms are associated with any red flags for intracranial or orbital pathology including: severe frontal headache, severe systemic symptoms, altered visual acuity or diplopia.
index.alternativeManagement: Rhinosinusitis symptoms associated with any of the following red flags for sino-nasal malignancy — refer for urgent ORL assessment: unilateral nasal obstruction with haemo-serous discharge; recent onset of Horner syndrome, diplopia, or cranial neuropathies (particularly sensory change); paraesthesia in the skin of cheek, maxillary gingiva, or hard palate. Allergic rhinitis causing nasal obstruction or discharge — see local Rhino-Sinusitis HealthPathway.
index.notFundedDetail: Follow-up scans to assess treatment response.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for follow-up scans to assess treatment response. Red flags for sino-nasal malignancy (unilateral nasal obstruction with haemo-serous discharge; Horner syndrome/diplopia/cranial neuropathies; paraesthesia of cheek/gingiva/palate) — refer urgently to ORL.

index.guidance: Opportunistic liver function testing is not indicated for asymptomatic people without risk factors.
index.guidanceNarrative: Opportunistic liver function testing is not indicated for asymptomatic people without risk factors. 2.5% of the normal population will fall outside the upper limit of the reference range as part of normal statistical distribution, and test results may fluctuate as a physiological response. Elevated liver enzyme levels are estimated to occur in up to 9% of asymptomatic people. Abnormal liver blood test results should only be interpreted after review of previous results, past medical history and current medical condition. Liver disease is rare among people with abnormal LFT results in primary care. On the other hand, severe liver disease may be present with little or no liver function abnormality, so the level or duration of elevation should not determine the significance, but the result should be viewed within the context of trend, past medical and drug history and current symptoms. For practical purposes, marker abnormalities are usually considered 'borderline' if they are less than 2 times the ULN and 'mild' if they are 2-5 times the ULN. If hepatocellular injury is suspected and the patient does not have concerning symptoms/features, ultrasound is a second-tier investigation after investigating the most likely causes. Obesity and raised ALT provide strong evidence for a presumptive diagnosis of fatty liver — ultrasound is not always necessary. Ultrasound imaging is a first-line investigation if cholestasis is suspected based on LFT results.
index.alternativeManagement: Upper abdominal non-pulsatile mass not consistent with hepatomegaly — discuss with PCRL or relevant secondary care specialist as referral for CT abdomen may be more appropriate. Unexplained weight loss, persistent fatigue, vague abdominal pain and suspicion of underlying malignancy — see local CT Chest, Abdomen and Pelvis HealthPathway.
index.notFundedDetail: Hernia in adults. Infective hepatitis. Follow-up imaging of previously diagnosed gallstones in asymptomatic patients. Isolated bloating (however with additional clinical symptoms such as loss of appetite, weight loss, abdominal pain or abnormal vaginal bleeding, USS or CT may be warranted). Follow-up imaging of simple renal or hepatic cysts. Screening for aortic aneurysm or fatty liver.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: hernia in adults; infective hepatitis; follow-up of previously diagnosed gallstones in asymptomatic patients; isolated bloating; follow-up of simple renal or hepatic cysts; screening for AAA or fatty liver.

index.guidance: All patients with suspected TIA who after specialist assessment are candidates for carotid endarterectomy should have urgent carotid imaging. Choice of modality (US, CTA, or MRA) based on local expertise. Carotid imaging preferably completed at same visit as brain imaging.
index.guidanceNarrative: Recommended best practice is for all patients with suspected TIA who after specialist assessment are considered as candidates for carotid endarterectomy to have urgent carotid imaging. The choice of modality (ultrasound, CTA, or MRA) should be based on local expertise and availability. Carotid imaging is preferably completed at the same visit as brain imaging.
index.alternativeManagement: High-risk TIAs (atypical symptoms, patient in atrial fibrillation and on anticoagulants) — request acute on-call stroke specialist assessment. Suspected TIA over 7 days ago — see local TIA HealthPathway.
index.notFundedDetail: Carotid endarterectomy is not publicly funded for asymptomatic stenosis so carotid imaging is not funded for: asymptomatic bruits; carotid artery calcification on X-ray; screening tests.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not funded for: asymptomatic bruits; carotid artery calcification on X-ray; screening tests. High risk TIAs (atypical symptoms, AF, anticoagulants) — request acute on-call stroke specialist assessment.

index.guidance: No routine direct access to publicly funded (non-ACC) musculoskeletal ultrasound. Ultrasound findings alone do not typically change management. Initial management should always include a 2-3 month trial of physiotherapy. Radiology is not a prerequisite for a trial of steroid injection when red flags are excluded.
index.guidanceNarrative: 70% of shoulder pain in general practice can be attributed to the rotator cuff complex. Rotator cuff tendonitis is the most common underlying pathology and most likely diagnosis in the absence of demonstrated weakness on examination. Most presentations with shoulder pain and suspected rotator cuff pathologies including smaller tears will resolve with conservative management and do not require imaging. Initial management should always include a 2-3 month trial of physiotherapy. Radiology is not a pre-requisite for a trial of steroid injection when a full clinical assessment has been undertaken and red flags excluded. There is no routine direct access to publicly funded (non-ACC) shoulder or other musculoskeletal ultrasound. Ultrasound findings alone do not typically change the management approach. If there is inadequate response to physiotherapy (+/- cortisone injection), management advice may be sought from secondary care and imaging requested on their recommendation or a case for imaging made to the PCRL.
index.alternativeManagement: Shoulder pain with reduced passive range of motion and frozen shoulder is suspected — see local Shoulder Pain HealthPathway. Suspected olecranon or other bursitis — see local Shoulder Pain HealthPathway.
index.notFundedDetail: Achilles or other tendon pain. Plantar fasciitis. Forefoot pain — suspected Morton's neuroma. Tennis elbow. Golfer's elbow. Tenosynovitis. Trochanteric pain (gluteal tendinopathy).
index.footnotes: *1 Red flags: fever/systemically unwell; persistent night pain waking from sleep; pain at rest; restricted passive movement; inflammation/swelling.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: Achilles or other tendon pain; plantar fasciitis; forefoot pain (suspected Morton's neuroma); tennis elbow; golfer's elbow; tenosynovitis; trochanteric pain (gluteal tendinopathy). index.guidance: For palpable masses, FNA (preferably US-guided) provides better diagnostic accuracy than US alone and is the preferred initial diagnostic approach. Ultrasound reveals nodules in up to 65% of adults — the large majority are benign.
index.guidanceNarrative: Ultrasound is of limited value in the diagnostic work up of thyroid and neck masses. It can help confirm the origin of the mass and provide some characterisation but for palpable masses, fine needle aspiration (FNA — preferably ultrasound-guided) provides better diagnostic accuracy than ultrasound alone and is the preferred initial diagnostic approach where available. Ultrasound evaluation of the thyroid reveals nodules in up to 65% of adults depending on age and gender. The large majority are benign nodules. Thyroid cancer occurs in 7-15% of thyroid nodules. 85% of these are papillary cancers which don't cause any ill-health or affect longevity and there is therefore no benefit in finding these cancers. Approximately 5% of thyroid nodules will harbour an important thyroid cancer (one likely to cause ill health in the future). The challenge is to identify the few clinically important cancers (medullary, anaplastic) but avoid using a lot of scarce resource working up all incidentally discovered nodules.
index.alternativeManagement: Multiple neck masses or suspected metastatic lymphadenopathy — investigate and refer as per the Neck Lumps in Adults HealthPathway. Incidental thyroid nodule on CT scan or MRI and TSH reduced — refer for nuclear medicine Tc-99m pertechnetate scan. Goitre or diffuse thyroid swelling with severe obstructive symptoms (stridor, wheezing at rest) — refer for acute secondary care assessment as per local HealthPathway. Goitre or thyroid mass with mild to moderate obstructive symptoms — see local Goitre HealthPathway. Thyrotoxicosis/Hyperthyroidism — see local Hyperthyroidism HealthPathway. Hypothyroidism — see local Hypothyroid HealthPathway. If presenting symptoms do not meet criteria but ultrasound may be indicated — seek advice from PCRL or relevant secondary care specialist.
index.notFundedDetail: ITN in patient with co-morbidities and limited life expectancy — imaging work up not recommended. Solitary ITN on CT or MR imaging smaller than 10mm in diameter and without adverse imaging or clinically suspicious features. Multiple nodules all smaller than 20mm in diameter and without any adverse features on CT or MR imaging and likely to be benign.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: ITN with co-morbidities and limited life expectancy; solitary ITN on CT/MR <10mm without adverse features; multiple nodules all <20mm without adverse features. Incidental thyroid nodule with reduced TSH — refer for nuclear medicine scan.

index.guidance: In acute pelvic pain, ultrasound as a rule-out investigation is limited — acuity of presentation should determine referral to hospital vs community management. PID is commonly associated with a normal ultrasound and is typically a clinical diagnosis. Maori and Pacific peoples are disproportionately affected by endometrial cancer — lower threshold for investigation.
index.guidanceNarrative: In the acute pelvic pain presentation, the use of ultrasound as a 'rule out' investigation is limited — the acuity of the presentation (and the expected management required) should remain the determining factor in referral to hospital vs community management. If the patient requires hospital assessment regardless of the ultrasound result, a community-provided acute demand ultrasound is typically not warranted unless specifically advised by a secondary care specialist. In suspected ovarian/adnexal torsion, ultrasound has good specificity (85-90%) but lower sensitivity (75-80%). Pelvic Inflammatory Disease (PID) remains the most common cause of acute pelvic pain. It is commonly associated with a normal ultrasound and is typically considered a clinical diagnosis. Māori and Pacific Peoples are disproportionately affected by endometrial cancer (EC) and endometrial hyperplasia (EH). Investigations including transvaginal ultrasound and/or aspiration endometrial sampling should be considered at or soon after initial presentation for all Māori and Pacific people presenting with abnormal uterine bleeding (AUB).
index.alternativeManagement: Suspected retained products of conception (RPOC) with persistent or heavy bleeding after abortion or miscarriage — see local Abortion HealthPathway or Miscarriage HealthPathway. Suspected endometriosis in the absence of a clinically palpable mass — see local Endometriosis HealthPathway. Primary dysmenorrhoea in the absence of a clinically palpable mass — see local Dysmenorrhoea HealthPathway. Pelvic inflammatory disease in the absence of a clinically palpable mass — see local PID HealthPathway. Cervical polyps — referral to secondary care rather than ultrasound is recommended for women with polyps which prove difficult to remove. Secondary amenorrhoea — pelvic USS is not normally helpful unless a pelvic mass is present or the patient has significantly raised androgens.
index.notFundedDetail: Routine follow-up of known fibroids. Subfertility. Follow-up of asymptomatic simple ovarian cyst (O-RADS 2) less than 3cm in diameter in post-menopausal woman. Follow-up of asymptomatic simple ovarian cyst (O-RADS 2) less than 5cm in diameter in premenopausal woman. Follow-up of asymptomatic non-simple ovarian cyst less than 3cm diameter in premenopausal woman. Follow-up of typical-appearing haemorrhagic cyst up to 5cm diameter in pre-menopausal woman. Benign-appearing endometrial cells on smear in asymptomatic pre-menopausal women with no abnormality on exam and without any risk factors for EH/EC. Erratic unscheduled bleeding in the first 3 to 6 months after starting MHT. Screening ultrasound for family history of ovarian cancer (refer to secondary care instead). Women aged less than 45 years with AUB and no risk factors for EH/EC, no abnormality on examination, and where first-line treatment has not been trialled.
index.footnotes:
*1 Patients with suspected endometritis: suspected infection with ongoing pelvic pain post-partum/miscarriage/abortion, uterine tenderness, with or without fever and/or bleeding but systemically stable. If systemically unstable and/or severe persistent pelvic pain refer for acute assessment without requesting imaging.
*2 Presentations warranting consideration of ovarian cancer in absence of palpable mass — new abdominal or pelvic symptoms on persistent or frequent basis (particularly more than 12 times per month): persistent abdominal distension or bloating; early satiety or loss of appetite; pelvic or abdominal pain without a known cause; increased urinary urgency or frequency; irritable bowel symptoms especially if new onset and aged over 50; unexplained weight loss or fatigue.
*3 Abnormal Uterine Bleeding (AUB): bleeding from the uterus that is abnormal in volume, regularity, frequency or duration in the absence of pregnancy (excluding postmenopausal bleeding). May be acute (urgent referral) or chronic (6 months or more). Includes heavy menstrual bleeding, irregular cycles (10+ days variation), prolonged menstrual bleeding (>8 days), secondary amenorrhoea, infrequent cycles (<every 38 days), frequent cycles (>every 24 days), intermenstrual bleeding, post-coital bleeding, and unscheduled bleeding on hormonal medication.
*4 Non-uterine causes of bleeding: vulvovaginal atrophy/atrophic vaginitis; vulval dermatoses; STIs including chlamydia; malignancy of cervix/vagina/vulva; cervical ectropion; urethral causes.
*5 Risk factors for EH/EC: BMI greater than 30; diabetes mellitus (Types I and II); Pacific and Māori ethnicity; PCOS and/or chronic anovulation; unopposed oestrogen therapy; nulliparity and older than 35; early menarche (less than 10 years old); family history of endometrial, ovarian, breast or bowel cancer; oestrogen-secreting tumour.
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: routine follow-up of known fibroids; subfertility; follow-up of asymptomatic simple ovarian cyst (O-RADS 2) <3cm post-menopausal or <5cm pre-menopausal; screening for ovarian cancer family history (refer to secondary care). Women aged <45 with AUB, no risk factors, no abnormality on exam, and first-line treatment not yet trialled.

index.alternativeManagement: Incidental finding on ultrasound of a solid lesion more than 1cm diameter with typical ultrasound appearances of an angiomyolipoma — see local CT IVU HealthPathway. Macroscopic haematuria aged 40 to 85 years without UTI — refer for CT IVU. Repeat renal ultrasound for diabetic patients with known diabetic renal complications is not indicated. Acute kidney injury — manage according to Acute Kidney Injury pathway or seek advice from relevant secondary care specialist. Enlarged prostate or elevated PSA — see local Prostate Cancer Diagnosis HealthPathway. Isolated proteinuria — seek nephrology advice. Lower urinary tract symptoms — see local LUTS or Benign Prostatic Hypertrophy HealthPathway.

Follow-up of incidental finding on ultrasound of a solid lesion less than 1cm diameter with typical appearances of an angiomyolipoma. Screening scan for Autosomal Dominant Polycystic Kidney Disease (ADPKD). Serial ultrasounds of known polycystic kidneys unless indicated by the radiology report or the patient develops new symptoms meeting criteria.

index.guidanceNarrative: There is wide variation in the amount of normal faecal residue shown on AXR — it does not reliably diagnose constipation nor should it be used for monitoring response to treatment. The sensitivity and specificity of AXR is low in both the diagnosis and identifying underlying cause of bowel obstruction. As a more definitive test, CT is preferable and AXR may result in delays to a more definitive test.

index.guidance: Osteoarthritis is typically a clinical diagnosis. X-rays reserved for persisting joint pain with features atypical for OA (patient <45; recent trauma; signs of inflammation; nocturnal pain; weight loss; inability to weight bear).
index.guidanceNarrative: Osteoarthritis is typically a clinical diagnosis and X-ray is not routinely needed to make the diagnosis. Imaging is also not required for effective non-surgical management of osteoarthritis. There is generally poor correlation between symptoms and X-ray changes — X-rays are poor indicators of the severity of disease except in advanced stages. X-rays to help with diagnosis are reserved for patients who present with persisting joint pain and clinical features atypical for osteoarthritis (e.g. younger age less than 45 years, pain at rest rather than with activity, persisting stiffness, systemic features).
index.alternativeManagement: Acute gout — see local Gout HealthPathway.
index.notFundedDetail: Clinically suspected osteoarthritis except as above. Bunions and bunionettes. Flat feet. Heel pain. Heel spurs. Plantar fasciitis. Suspected Sever's Disease.

index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: clinically suspected OA (except above); bunions; flat feet; heel pain/spurs; plantar fasciitis; Sever's disease; acute gout.

index.guidance: Approximately 90% of acute low back pain in primary care is benign and self-limiting. Between 1-4% will have a spinal fracture and <1% malignancy. Accurate screening for red flags is paramount.
index.guidanceNarrative: Approximately 90% of acute low back pain episodes presenting to primary care are benign self-limiting cases that do not warrant x-ray or other imaging studies. Spinal fracture and malignancy are the most common serious pathologies affecting the spine. In patients with low back pain presenting to primary care, between 1% and 4% will have a spinal fracture and in less than 1% malignancy. The low prevalence of both in primary care settings does not justify routine ancillary testing. Accurate screening tools to aid clinical decisions about when to refer for further testing +/- imaging are paramount.
index.alternativeManagement: History of recent trauma — request imaging via ACC funding. Progressive neurological deficit — seek Orthopaedic advice.
index.notFundedDetail: Acute (within the first six weeks) uncomplicated spine pain without red flags — see local Back Pain in Adults HealthPathway. Suspected spinal osteoarthritis or chronic spine pain without red flags (systemic illness, suspicion of malignancy, risk of infection, inflammatory disease, or osteoporosis).
index.outOfCriteriaNote: Not routinely funded for: acute uncomplicated spine pain within first 6 weeks without red flags (see Back Pain in Adults HealthPathway); suspected spinal OA or chronic spine pain without red flags. History of recent trauma — request imaging via ACC funding.

items.label: New lump on trunk or limbs with any concerning features: deep to fascia, fixed, hard, or irregular of any size; larger than 5cm; rapidly changing; painful; recurring after previous excision
items.short: New trunk/limb lump with concerning features (deep/fixed/large/rapid/recurrent)

items.label: Secondary care clinician or PCRL or radiologist advises referral for urgent ultrasound
items.short: Advised by secondary care / PCRL / radiologist (urgent)

items.label: Secondary care clinician or PCRL or radiologist advises referral for non-urgent ultrasound
items.short: Advised by secondary care / PCRL / radiologist (non-urgent)

paed_index.guidance: Most ingested foreign bodies are low risk and can be managed without imaging. X-rays when required should include neck, chest, and abdomen with lateral as well as frontal views of neck and chest.
paed_index.notFundedDetail: Abdominal pain other than suspected constipation refractory to initial management. Single episode of vomiting without red flags.
paed_index.outOfCriteriaNote: Abdominal pain other than suspected constipation refractory to initial management. Single episode of vomiting without red flags.

groups.title: Refer for acute assessment — without initial imaging

items.label: Foreign body ingestion with: suspected oesophageal obstruction (drooling or reluctance to swallow); known or suspected disc/button battery ingestion; multiple magnets ingested; large object more than 5cm long or 2cm wide
items.short: Foreign body ingestion with red flags (battery, magnet, large object, or oesophageal obstruction)

items.label: Acute abdomen
items.short: Acute abdomen

items.short: Suspected foreign body ingestion — asymptomatic, no red flags, X-ray advised by secondary care

items.label: Secondary care clinician, PCRL or radiologist advises referral for non-urgent X-ray
items.short: Advised by secondary care / PCRL / radiologist (non-urgent)

paed_index.guidance: Chest X-ray is rarely required in suspected community-acquired pneumonia. Consider if: diagnostic uncertainty or atypical presentation; not making expected clinical progress; recurrent pneumonia symptoms suggesting underlying respiratory disease.
paed_index.notFundedDetail: Asthma — chest X-ray is not typically indicated for routine asthma management. Simple viral respiratory tract infection in a well child.
paed_index.outOfCriteriaNote: Asthma — chest X-ray is not typically indicated for routine asthma management. Simple viral respiratory tract infection in a well child.

paed_index.guidance: Red flags for septic arthritis or osteomyelitis: single painful or swollen limb; night waking with pain; unable to move limb normally; unwell with fever without obvious cause; skin infection slow to resolve especially over a joint.
paed_index.notFundedDetail: Pulled elbow (radial head subluxation) — diagnosed and treated clinically without imaging in most cases.
paed_index.outOfCriteriaNote: Pulled elbow (radial head subluxation) — diagnosed and treated clinically without imaging in most cases.

paed_index.guidance: All children with knee pain need exclusion of hip pathology. If no knee pathology on examination, arrange hip X-ray with AP pelvis and frog lateral view.

paed_index.outOfCriteriaNote: Knock knees (genu valgum) without pathological cause in child under 2.5 years. Osgood-Schlatter disease — usually clinical diagnosis.

paed_index.guidance: X-ray of the pelvis becomes the preferred investigation for DDH after age 4–6 months when ossification obscures ultrasound landmarks. Perthes disease and slipped upper femoral epiphysis (SUFE) should be considered in children with hip or knee pain.

paed_index.outOfCriteriaNote: Routine DDH screening X-ray in children with no risk factors and normal clinical examination.
